title: Quick check: connecting M365 for ITDR

questions.prompt: Huntress supports two ITDR integration paths for Microsoft 365. What are they?
choices: Direct tenant mapping (per-tenant Global Admin consent) and GDAP/CSP-based mapping (MSP service account via Partner Center).

choices.label: Read-only mapping and read-write mapping.
choices.rationale: Wrong axis. Both paths grant the same documented scope set; the difference is WHO authenticates.

choices.label: Self-hosted and SaaS.
choices.rationale: Huntress is SaaS-only; no self-hosted path exists.

choices.label: Cloud and on-prem.
choices.rationale: Identity-side integration is cloud-only. On-prem isn't an option here.

questions.explanation: The two paths land in the same place, telemetry flowing; the difference is who needs to be present at the connection step.

questions.prompt: Which of these is a signal that your MSP uses the GDAP/CSP path rather than the direct path?
choices: Your MSP uses Microsoft Partner Center to manage customer tenants, and the runbook references a "Huntress service account."

choices.label: The runbook says to schedule with the customer's Global Admin.
choices.rationale: That's the direct path, a customer-side admin consent is required.

choices.label: The customer has fewer than 50 endpoints.
choices.rationale: Size doesn't determine path; MSP delivery model does.

choices.label: The integration is being run on a weekday.
choices.rationale: Not a determinant.

questions.explanation: Service account plus Partner Center is the GDAP signature; the customer's consent happened earlier when they accepted the GDAP relationship.

questions.prompt: Onboarding ticket says "Standard onboarding, ITDR M365 included." You start scheduling a call with the customer's IT manager. Your senior pings: "Why are you booking a call?" What's the right interpretation?
choices: Re-check the runbook. If it's the GDAP path, the connection doesn't need a customer Global Admin and the call is unnecessary.

choices.label: The senior is being controlling, push back; the call is part of the runbook.
choices.rationale: The senior asking that question usually means your MSP uses GDAP, or the senior owns the connection step.

choices.label: Tell the senior the call is needed and book it anyway.
choices.rationale: Worst response, overcommits customer time for something the senior might be about to tell you they own.

questions.explanation: Two minutes of reading saves an awkward customer call. Read first, push back later if it's still warranted.

questions.prompt: The Identity → Microsoft 365 page reads "Connected" with green status. Is the integration done?

choices.label: Yes, connected is the success state.
choices.rationale: Incomplete. The status itself can show green while the integration is half-installed.

choices: Not yet, also verify the last-sync timestamp is moving, and that ingested identity events appear within 24-48 hours. A 'Connected' status without event flow is a half-installed state.

choices.label: No, wait for the customer's first identity incident before declaring success.
choices.rationale: Too long. Events flow within 24-48 hours; incident landing is downstream.

choices.label: No, Huntress support has to confirm telemetry receipt.
choices.rationale: Verification is portal-side, not support-side.

questions.explanation: Connected is necessary; events flowing is sufficient. A stale last-sync is the early sign of a half-installed pipeline.

questions.prompt: The senior confirms your MSP uses GDAP-based integration and that the connection step itself is senior-only. They ask you to do everything else. What do you do?

choices.label: Push back; the runbook implies the tech runs the whole thing.
choices.rationale: Arguing for broader scope is asking for permissions you haven't been given.

choices: Create the org per lesson 2, confirm the blank state and the GDAP-relationship prerequisite, schedule the 48-hour verification check. Hand the connection itself to the senior.

choices.label: Skip everything; the senior owns the integration.
choices.rationale: Wrong in the opposite direction. Verification, org-create, and customer comms are still yours.

questions.explanation: Clean scope split. You do the parts you own and give the senior a clean starting state.

questions.prompt: "Stopping at 'Connected' without verifying telemetry flow." Why does this matter?
choices: It leaves a quietly half-integrated tenant that doesn't produce identity incidents. The later identity-incident work has no signal to feed it.

choices.label: Huntress support audits Connected-but-quiet tenants and flags the MSP.
choices.rationale: Not the lesson's reason.

choices.label: The Connected status will roll back to disconnected if no events flow.
choices.rationale: The status stays green, which is exactly why the verify step matters.

choices.label: There's no real risk, the system will catch up eventually.
choices.rationale: Inverts the lesson's warning.

questions.explanation: Connected without events is the worst of both worlds: looks fine on the dashboard, produces no identity protection.

questions.prompt: The customer asks to reduce the scope of consent or the GDAP roles Huntress requires. What's the right move?
choices: Bump to a senior, product-configuration question. The auto-response actions are part of ITDR's value; the role set is calibrated for the integration.

choices.label: Negotiate a custom scope with the customer on the call to keep them moving.
choices.rationale: Scope changes commit the customer and the product team to a non-standard configuration the tech doesn't own.

choices.label: Refuse outright; the scopes are non-negotiable.
choices.rationale: Lecture-mode response. The senior may have framing or alternatives a tech doesn't see.

choices.label: Reduce the scopes in Partner Center yourself and continue.
choices.rationale: Way above the helpdesk floor.

questions.explanation: Scope decisions are product configuration, senior-owned. Bump cleanly with the specific request.

questions.prompt: On the direct path: the Global Admin tries to consent but the screen says "approval required." What is most likely happening, and what do you do?
choices: The customer's M365 has restricted third-party admin consents. Don't talk them into changing the tenant policy on the spot, bump.

choices.label: The Global Admin isn't actually a Global Admin, ask them to escalate their role.
choices.rationale: 'Approval required' is a policy gate, not a role-mismatch error.

choices.label: The Huntress enterprise app isn't published, open a Huntress support ticket.
choices.rationale: The app is published; the gate is on the customer's side.

choices.label: Retry until the screen accepts the consent, it usually clears.
choices.rationale: The gate doesn't clear on retry; it clears when the customer's tenant policy permits it.

questions.explanation: Tenant admin-consent policy is deliberate; renegotiating it on a connection call is above the helpdesk floor.

questions.prompt: "Your MSP's runbook puts the connection step above the helpdesk floor." How should you read this lesson?
choices: Read-to-understand, not read-to-execute. Knowing how the integration works still matters for customer comms, identity-incident work, and recognising half-connected states, but you don't run the connection itself.

choices.label: Skip the lesson; you'll never need this.
choices.rationale: You still work identity incidents from the resulting telemetry.

choices.label: Read it carefully so you can run the connection if a senior isn't available.
choices.rationale: The ceiling doesn't lift when a senior isn't available.

choices.label: Read it and immediately ask the senior to delegate the step to you.
choices.rationale: The scoping is deliberate (credentials, GDAP-role management).

questions.explanation: The scoping is deliberate. Read-to-understand still matters; the work you own around the connection is real.

questions.prompt: The direct path and the GDAP path grant the same documented set of scopes; the difference is who authenticates at the connection step.
questions.explanation: Both paths land in the same place, the same telemetry flows. The difference is the customer's Global Admin (direct) versus an MSP service account via Partner Center (GDAP).
